headline: Cost of Living Crisis Pushes 600,000 Into B40 Bracket
context: DOSM's 2025 Household Income Survey revealed 600,000 households slipped from M40 into B40 classification between 2022 and 2025, driven by stagnant wages and persistent inflation above 3.5%.

cards.big: 600,000 families fell from M40 to B40 in 3 years — Malaysia's middle class is shrinking measurably
cards.sub: B40 income ceiling rose to RM5,580 but real purchasing power fell 8.3% over same period

cards.big: Median household income grew 2.1% annually since 2022 while consumer prices rose 3.7% per year
cards.sub: Real wage stagnation means average Malaysian worker earns less in purchasing power than in 2019

cards.big: Household debt-to-GDP at 82.4% is second highest in Asia — RM1.53 trillion in total household borrowing
cards.sub: Average household spends 48% of monthly income on debt repayment including housing and vehicles

cards.big: DOSM changed B40/M40/T20 methodology 3 times since 2019 making trend comparison difficult
cards.sub: Income classification opacity allows government to claim poverty reduction even as living standards fall

cards.big: When 600,000 families slide backwards in 3 years, it's not a statistic — it's a national emergency
Should Malaysia adopt an absolute poverty line indexed to actual costs instead of relative income brackets?
